summary: Riccomini critiques Google Cloud's handling of a major outage that deleted UniSuper's private cloud for over a week due to a misconfigured parameter. He argues the incident reveals three systemic problems: Google's fundamental misunderstanding of enterprise customers, their superficial incident remediation that only addressed the proximate cause, and growing brand damage as Google Cloud gains a reputation for instability. Despite being a self-professed Google Cloud fan, he finds their incident response tone-deaf—notably lacking any apology while defensively claiming to be 'the most resilient cloud infrastructure in the world.'
key_points: Google's incident response contained no apology and defensively emphasized the outage only affected one customer, revealing a fundamental disconnect with enterprise customer expectations
The remediation was dangerously superficial—only addressing the specific deletion flow rather than auditing other tools, products, and deletion workflows across the organization
Enterprise customers expect staged deletions before hard-deleting anything, and more than a 'terse single-page remediation'
Google Cloud is developing a reputation for instability, with even Google's own Waymo reportedly not using it for infrastructure
Google historically ran their cloud product like consumer software, even A/B testing admin interfaces during production outages
money_quotes: It's important to talk about a company's failures as well as their successes.
Nowhere does Google's post even apologize. In fact, the only apology I can find is on UniSuper's website.
While this may be true, I find it completely tone-deaf. So much for customer empathy.
Enterprises expect more than a terse single-page remediation with no apology.
You don't want to A/B test user interfaces in an organization when they're in the middle of a production outage.
Google could have audited their other deletion flows, their other tools, and their other products. They could have modified deletion workflows to stage deletions. Instead, they did none of this. Why?
Google's remediation indicates that they don't understand how damaging this outage was to their brand.
If Google won't use their cloud service, what business do you have selling it to others?
key_insight: Google Cloud's dismissive incident response and superficial remediation reveal a company that still treats enterprise customers like consumer software users, failing to understand that trust—not just technical capability—is what enterprises are buying.
